participants.extracted: The study enrolled 30 male patients diagnosed with coronary heart disease (CHD), aged 30 to 65 years, recruited from inpatient and outpatient services at St. Johns Medical College and Hospital, Bangalore. Participants were randomized to either a mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) group (n=15; mean age 47.27 years, SD 12.15) or a treatment-as-usual (TAU) group (n=15; mean age 50.60 years, SD 8.21). Inclusion criteria required hospitalization or cardiac symptoms within the prior year, echocardiography showing ejection fraction >35%, and the ability to read, write, and speak English. Women were not included because most declined participation due to transportation, childcare, and other responsibilities. Individuals with psychoses, obsessive-compulsive disorder, mental retardation, mania, severe depression, neurological or serious medical conditions, or prior/current psychological intervention were excluded.

interventions.extracted: The intervention was a Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) program based on Kabat-Zinn and Segal et al., delivered individually over 8 weekly sessions lasting 1 to 1.5 hours each. It included training in body scan meditation, sitting meditation, mindful walking, mindful eating, the 3-minute breathing space, mastery and pleasure activities, and cognitive restructuring. Participants also received an audio cassette with guided instructions for mindfulness meditation and body scan meditation and were asked to practice 30 minutes of meditation at home each day. Both groups received health education about coronary heart disease and routine cardiac care, including medical management and monthly cardiology follow-up.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure psychological and physical outcomes at baseline (pre-assessment), immediately after the 8–10 week intervention (post-assessment), and again at 3-month follow-up. Psychological outcomes included anxiety symptoms and depressive symptoms, assessed with the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS), with separate anxiety and depression subscales and a total HADS distress score, and perceived stress, assessed with the Perceived Stress Scale (PSS). Physical outcomes included blood pressure, measured by sphygmomanometer and reported as systolic blood pressure (and blood pressure overall as a clinical physical parameter), and body mass index (BMI), calculated from anthropometric measurement. These outcomes were collected in both the mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) group and the treatment-as-usual group at pre-assessment and post-assessment, with follow-up assessment for available participants 3 months after post-assessment.
